name: Lentils
summary: A fast-cooking pulse packing plant protein, fiber, iron, and folate — with meta-analysed evidence for lowering LDL cholesterol.
superfoodReason: A cheap, quick-cooking pulse combining protein, soluble fiber, iron, and folate, with pooled-trial evidence for improving cholesterol.

benefits.mechanism: Lentils' soluble fiber binds bile acids in the gut, so the liver pulls cholesterol from the blood to make more — lowering LDL. A meta-analysis of randomized trials found dietary pulses (lentils, beans, chickpeas) reduced LDL cholesterol.

citations.title: Effect of dietary pulse intake on established therapeutic lipid targets for cardiovascular risk reduction: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials (Ha et al., CMAJ 2014)

benefits.mechanism: Lentils supply non-heme (plant) iron for red blood cells — best absorbed alongside a vitamin C source — and are rich in folate, the B-vitamin needed to build DNA and new cells.

citations.title: Folate — NIH Office of Dietary Supplements (Health Professional Fact Sheet)

surprisingFacts.text: Unlike most dried beans, lentils need no soaking and cook in about 20 minutes — red lentils collapse into a purée, green and black ones hold their shape.

cautions.effect: Gas and bloating from the fibre; they need no soaking but should be cooked through.
